Minds that made money · 13
He saw the India tech story before it was a story —
and held it longer than anyone thought was rational.
Then, at the peak, they banned him from the markets for five years.
Meet the OG of Indian fund managers →
02
Who
Samir
Arora
Founder & CIO, Helios Capital. Ran Alliance's India business 1993–2003. Early-to-mid 60s, IIT Delhi '83. Backed the India growth story for three decades.
~24% a year
across his stitched track record — vs the Nifty's ~13%.
Bio: Helios Capital; IIT Delhi 1983. ~24% vs ~13% is a stitched, illustrative blend (Alliance 1996–2003 + Helios 2005–2023, gap years index-bridged), single secondary source — Moneycontrol via TradingView, 2023.

“
Why can't we own two contrasting ideas — a stock that offers incredible growth and another that is incredibly cheap.
Samir AroraHe never confined himself to one box.
What most people miss →
05
He didn't diamond-hand it.
He believed in the theme and churned ruthlessly inside it.
The conviction was India. The holdings got cut fast. "Held longer than rational" is the index — not his style.
06
One question
Is your conviction in the story — or just in the stock you happen to hold?
One you can keep through a drawdown. One you have to defend every morning.
07
He didn't out-trade the market.
He out-believed it.
Banned at his peak, exonerated, back at ~24% a year for two more decades.
Now you've got
AI on your side.

Education only. Not investment advice. ~24% vs ~13% is a stitched, illustrative blend. Sources: SAT order (2004); Moneycontrol via TradingView; Helios Capital; Business Today (2023).

Caption — Instagram post body

In 2003, at the absolute peak of his career, the regulator banned Samir Arora from the markets for five years.

He'd run Alliance's India business since 1993 — backing the India growth story before most people thought India was investable. Then came an interim SEBI order over alleged insider trading and disclosure lapses. Most people, written off like that, fade out.

He fought it. The Securities Appellate Tribunal threw the case out — in its own words, "exonerating him honourably of all the charges." Then in 2005 he started over, founded Helios, and kept betting on the same story. Early into Zomato, Paytm, Policybazaar. Roughly 24% a year across his stitched track record, against about 13% for the Nifty.

Here's the part the highlight reel skips: this wasn't diamond-hands. His conviction was in the theme — India compounding — not in any one stock. Inside that, he churned ruthlessly, cut losers fast, sat out rallies he didn't believe in, and was wrong and early in stretches. The patience was with the story. The discipline was with the positions.

Worth asking yourself: is your conviction in the story you can hold through a drawdown — or just in the ticker you happen to own right now?

Save it for the next time the market makes you want to sell everything. Send it to someone holding on for the wrong reason.

(One honesty note: that ~24% vs ~13% is a stitched, illustrative blend across two funds with the gap years bridged by index returns, traced to a single source. Treat it as roughly double the index, not a clean audited number.)

Education only. Not investment advice.
Sources: SAT order (2004) · Moneycontrol via TradingView · Helios Capital · Business Today.
